CS-404 Big Data Analytics

  Week 13 ÔÇô Clustering

    Dr. Syed Imran Ali

                            15/04/2026
´éí Given a cloud of data points we want to
 understand its structure




           J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   2
´éí Given a set of points, with a notion of distance
 between points, group the points into some
 number of clusters, so that
  ´éž Members of a cluster are close/similar to each other
  ´éž Members of different clusters are dissimilar
´éí Usually:
  ´éž Points are in a high-dimensional space
  ´éž Similarity is defined using a distance measure
    ´éž Euclidean, Cosine, Jaccard, edit distance, ÔÇŽ


               J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   3
                                                   x
                                                                           x
                                                                     xx x
                                                                    x x
           x     x                                                  x x x
         x x x x                                                        x
         x xx x                                                      xx x
          x x x                                                         x
             x x



                      x                              x x
                                                 x x x x
                                                  x x x
                                                    x
Outlier                                                                                  Cluster

 J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org             4
J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   5
´éí Clustering in two dimensions looks easy
´éí Clustering small amounts of data looks easy
´éí And in most cases, looks are not deceiving


´éí Many applications involve not 2, but 10 or
  10,000 dimensions
´éí High-dimensional spaces look different:
  Almost all pairs of points are at about the
  same distance

            J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   6
´éí A catalog of 2 billion ÔÇťsky objectsÔÇŁ represents
  objects by their radiation in 7 dimensions
  (frequency bands)
´éí Problem: Cluster into similar objects, e.g.,
  galaxies, nearby stars, quasars, etc.
´éí Sloan Digital Sky Survey




            J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   7
´éí Intuitively: Music divides into categories, and
 customers prefer a few categories
  ´éž But what are categories really?

´éí Represent a CD by a set of customers who
 bought it:


´éí Similar CDs have similar sets of customers,
 and vice-versa

              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   8
Space of all CDs:
´éí Think of a space with one dim. for each
  customer
  ´éž Values in a dimension may be 0 or 1 only
  ´éž A CD is a point in this space (x1, x2,ÔÇŽ, xk),
    where xi = 1 iff the i th customer bought the CD

´éí For Amazon, the dimension is tens of millions

´éí Task: Find clusters of similar CDs


              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   9
Finding topics:
´éí Represent a document by a vector
  (x1, x2,ÔÇŽ, xk), where xi = 1 iff the i th word
  (in some order) appears in the document
  ´éž It actually doesnÔÇÖt matter if k is infinite; i.e., we
    donÔÇÖt limit the set of words

´éí Documents with similar sets of words
  may be about the same topic


               J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   10
´éí As with CDs we have a choice when we
 think of documents as sets of words or
 shingles:
 ´éž Sets as vectors: Measure similarity by the
   cosine distance
 ´éž Sets as sets: Measure similarity by the
   Jaccard distance
 ´éž Sets as points: Measure similarity by
   Euclidean distance


             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   11
´éí Hierarchical:
  ´éž Agglomerative (bottom up):
    ´éž Initially, each point is a cluster
    ´éž Repeatedly combine the two
      ÔÇťnearestÔÇŁ clusters into one
  ´éž Divisive (top down):
    ´éž Start with one cluster and recursively split it

´éí Point assignment:
  ´éž Maintain a set of clusters
  ´éž Points belong to ÔÇťnearestÔÇŁ cluster
                J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   12
´éí Key operation:
 Repeatedly combine
 two nearest clusters

´éí Three important questions:
 ´éž 1) How do you represent a cluster of more
   than one point?
 ´éž 2) How do you determine the ÔÇťnearnessÔÇŁ of
   clusters?
 ´éž 3) When to stop combining clusters?

            J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   13
´éí Key operation: Repeatedly combine two
  nearest clusters
´éí (1) How to represent a cluster of many points?
  ´éž Key problem: As you merge clusters, how do you
    represent the ÔÇťlocationÔÇŁ of each cluster, to tell which
    pair of clusters is closest?
´éí Euclidean case: each cluster has a
  centroid = average of its (data)points
´éí (2) How to determine ÔÇťnearnessÔÇŁ of clusters?
  ´éž Measure cluster distances by distances of centroids

              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   14
                                                                     (5,3)
                                                                    o
      (1,2)
     o
          x (1.5,1.5)                                           x (4.7,1.3)
      x (1,1) o (2,1)                            o (4,1)
                                                      x (4.5,0.5)
o (0,0)                                                    o (5,0)




           Data:
           o ÔÇŽ data point
           x ÔÇŽ centroid
                                                                                                   Dendrogram
               J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org            15
What about the Non-Euclidean case?
´éí The only ÔÇťlocationsÔÇŁ we can talk about are the
  points themselves
  ´éž i.e., there is no ÔÇťaverageÔÇŁ of two points

´éí Approach 1:
  ´éž (1) How to represent a cluster of many points?
    clustroid = (data)point ÔÇťclosestÔÇŁ to other points
  ´éž (2) How do you determine the ÔÇťnearnessÔÇŁ of
    clusters? Treat clustroid as if it were centroid, when
    computing inter-cluster distances
              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   16
 ´éí (1) How to represent a cluster of many points?
   clustroid = point ÔÇťclosestÔÇŁ to other points
 ´éí Possible meanings of ÔÇťclosestÔÇŁ:
    ´éž Smallest maximum distance to other points
    ´éž Smallest average distance to other points
    ´éž Smallest sum of squares of distances to other points
       ´éž For distance metric d clustroid c of cluster C is: min
                                                             c
                                                                ´âą d ( x, c )
                                                                                                           x´âÄC
                                                                                                                           2


Datapoint                            Centroid

                     X                                                              Centroid is the avg. of all (data)points
                                                                                    in the cluster. This means centroid is
                              Clustroid                                             an ÔÇťartificialÔÇŁ point.
             Cluster on                                                             Clustroid is an existing (data)point
            3 datapoints                                                            that is ÔÇťclosestÔÇŁ to all other points in
                                                                                    the
                      J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets,     cluster.
                                                                                        http://www.mmds.org                 17
´éí (2) How do you determine the ÔÇťnearnessÔÇŁ of
 clusters?
 ´éž Approach 2:
   Intercluster distance = minimum of the distances
   between any two points, one from each cluster
 ´éž Approach 3:
   Pick a notion of ÔÇťcohesionÔÇŁ of clusters, e.g.,
   maximum distance from the clustroid
   ´éž Merge clusters whose union is most cohesive



             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   18
´éí Approach 3.1: Use the diameter of the
  merged cluster = maximum distance between
  points in the cluster
´éí Approach 3.2: Use the average distance
  between points in the cluster
´éí Approach 3.3: Use a density-based approach
 ´éž Take the diameter or avg. distance, e.g., and divide
   by the number of points in the cluster



             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   19
´éí Na├»ve implementation of hierarchical
 clustering:
 ´éž At each step, compute pairwise distances
   between all pairs of clusters, then merge
 ´éž O(N3)

´éí Careful implementation using priority queue
 can reduce time to O(N2 log N)
 ´éž Still too expensive for really big datasets
   that do not fit in memory

             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   20
´éí Assumes Euclidean space/distance

´éí Start by picking k, the number of clusters

´éí Initialize clusters by picking one point per
  cluster
  ´éž Example: Pick one point at random, then k-1
    other points, each as far away as possible from
    the previous points



             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   22
´éí 1) For each point, place it in the cluster whose
  current centroid it is nearest
´éí 2) After all points are assigned, update the
  locations of centroids of the k clusters
´éí 3) Reassign all points to their closest centroid
  ´éž Sometimes moves points between clusters

´éí Repeat 2 and 3 until convergence
  ´éž Convergence: Points donÔÇÖt move between clusters
    and centroids stabilize
             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   23
                                                                                    x
                                                                               x
                                                                         x
                                                                    x
                                                              x

           x      x         x           x       x       x




x ÔÇŽ data point
  ÔÇŽ centroid                                                                  Clusters after round 1

                 J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   24
                                                                                    x
                                                                               x
                                                                         x
                                                                    x
                                                              x

           x      x         x           x       x       x




x ÔÇŽ data point
  ÔÇŽ centroid                                                                  Clusters after round 2

                 J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   25
                                                                                    x
                                                                               x
                                                                         x
                                                                    x
                                                              x

           x      x         x           x       x       x




x ÔÇŽ data point
  ÔÇŽ centroid                                                                  Clusters at the end

                 J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   26
How to select k?
´éí Try different k, looking at the change in the
  average distance to centroid as k increases
´éí Average falls rapidly until right k, then
  changes little

                                                                      Best value
                                                                      of k
                         Average
                        distance to
                         centroid                                 k



             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   27
Too few;                                                         x
many long                                                                                x
                                                                                   xx x
distances
                                                                                  x x
to centroid.             x     x                                                  x x x
                       x x x x                                                        x
                       x xx x                                                      xx x

                                                               x x x x
                                                                x x x
                                                                  x


               J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   28

distances                                                                           xx x
rather short.                                                                      x x

                            x x

                                                                x x x x

Too many;                                                              x
little improvement                                                                             x
in average                                                                               xx x
distance.                                                                               x x

                             x x x x                                                        x
                             x xx x                                                      xx x
                              x x x                                                         x
                                 x x

                                                                     x x x x
                                                                      x x x
                                                                        x


                     J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   30
Extension of k-means to large data
´éí BFR [Bradley-Fayyad-Reina] is a
 variant of k-means designed to
 handle very large (disk-resident) data sets

´éí Assumes that clusters are normally distributed
 around a centroid in a Euclidean space
  ´éž Standard deviations in different
    dimensions may vary
    ´éž Clusters are axis-aligned ellipses
´éí Efficient way to summarize clusters
 (want memory required O(clusters) and not O(data))
                J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   32
´éí Points are read from disk one main-memory-
  full at a time
´éí Most points from previous memory loads are
  summarized by simple statistics
´éí To begin, from the initial load we select the
  initial k centroids by some sensible approach:
  ´éž Take k random points
  ´éž Take a small random sample and cluster optimally
  ´éž Take a sample; pick a random point, and then
    kÔÇô1 more points, each as far from the previously
    selected points as possible
             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   33
3 sets of points which we keep track of:
´éí Discard set (DS):
  ´éž Points close enough to a centroid to be
    summarized
´éí Compression set (CS):
  ´éž Groups of points that are close together but
    not close to any existing centroid
  ´éž These points are summarized, but not
    assigned to a cluster
´éí Retained set (RS):
  ´éž Isolated points waiting to be assigned to a
    compression set
              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   34
                                                                                                                Points in
                                                                                                                the RS



                                 Compressed sets.
                                 Their points are in
                                 the CS.




A cluster. Its points                                           The centroid
are in the DS.

                         Discard set (DS): Close enough to a centroid to be summarized
                         Compression set (CS): Summarized, but not assigned to a cluster
                         Retained set (RS): Isolated points
                        J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org               35
For each cluster, the discard set (DS) is
summarized by:
´éí The number of points, N
´éí The vector SUM, whose ith component is the
  sum of the coordinates of the points in the
  ith dimension
´éí The vector SUMSQ: ith component = sum of
  squares of coordinates in ith dimension

                                                    A cluster.
                                                    All its points are in the DS.                   The centroid
            J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org            36
  ´éí 2d + 1 values represent any size cluster
       ´éž d = number of dimensions
  ´éí Average in each dimension (the centroid)
       can be calculated as SUMi / N
       ´éž SUMi = ith component of SUM
  ´éí Variance of a clusterÔÇÖs discard set in
       dimension i is: (SUMSQi / N) ÔÇô (SUMi / N)2
       ´éž And standard deviation is the square root of that
  ´éí Next step: Actual clustering
Note: Dropping the ÔÇťaxis-alignedÔÇŁ clusters assumption would require
storing full covariance matrix to summarize the cluster. So, instead of
SUMSQ being a d-dim vector, it would be a d x d matrix, which is too big!
                             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   37
Processing the ÔÇťMemory-LoadÔÇŁ of points (1):
´éí 1) Find those points that are ÔÇťsufficiently
  closeÔÇŁ to a cluster centroid and add those
  points to that cluster and the DS
  ´éž These points are so close to the centroid that
    they can be summarized and then discarded
´éí 2) Use any main-memory clustering algorithm
 to cluster the remaining points and the old RS
  ´éž Clusters go to the CS; outlying points to the RS
               Discard set (DS): Close enough to a centroid to be summarized.
               Compression set (CS): Summarized, but not assigned to a cluster
               Retained set (RS): Isolated points
              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   38
Processing the ÔÇťMemory-LoadÔÇŁ of points (2):
´éí 3) DS set: Adjust statistics of the clusters to
  account for the new points
  ´éž Add Ns, SUMs, SUMSQs
´éí 4) Consider merging compressed sets in the CS

´éí 5) If this is the last round, merge all compressed
  sets in the CS and all RS points into their nearest
  cluster
              Discard set (DS): Close enough to a centroid to be summarized.
              Compression set (CS): Summarized, but not assigned to a cluster
              Retained set (RS): Isolated points
             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   39
                                                                                                                Points in
                                                                                                                the RS



                                 Compressed sets.
                                 Their points are in
                                 the CS.




A cluster. Its points                                           The centroid
are in the DS.

                         Discard set (DS): Close enough to a centroid to be summarized
                         Compression set (CS): Summarized, but not assigned to a cluster
                         Retained set (RS): Isolated points
                        J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org               40
´éí Q1) How do we decide if a point is ÔÇťclose
 enoughÔÇŁ to a cluster that we will add the
 point to that cluster?

´éí Q2) How do we decide whether two
 compressed sets (CS) deserve to be
 combined into one?




            J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   41
´éí Q1) We need a way to decide whether to put
 a new point into a cluster (and discard)

´éí BFR suggests two ways:
 ´éž The Mahalanobis distance is less than a threshold
 ´éž High likelihood of the point belonging to
   currently nearest centroid




            J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   42
´éí Normalized Euclidean distance from centroid

´éí For point (x1, ÔÇŽ, xd) and centroid (c1, ÔÇŽ, cd)
  1. Normalize in each dimension: yi = (xi - ci) / ´ü│i
  2. Take sum of the squares of the yi
  3. Take the square root
                                               ­ŁĹĹ                                    2
                                              ­ŁĹą­ŁĹľ Ôłĺ ­ŁĹÉ­ŁĹľ
            ­ŁĹĹ ­ŁĹą, ­ŁĹÉ =                        ÓĚŹ
                                                 ­ŁťÄ­ŁĹľ
                                            ­ŁĹľ=1
                                                                ¤âi ÔÇŽ standard deviation of points in
                                                                the cluster in the ith dimension
              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org    43
´éí If clusters are normally distributed in d
  dimensions, then after transformation, one
  standard deviation = ­Łĺů
  ´éž i.e., 68% of the points of the cluster will
    have a Mahalanobis distance < ­Łĺů

´éí Accept a point for a cluster if
  its M.D. is < some threshold,
  e.g. 2 standard deviations


              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   44
Q2) Should 2 CS subclusters be combined?
´éí Compute the variance of the combined
  subcluster
 ´éž N, SUM, and SUMSQ allow us to make that
   calculation quickly
´éí Combine if the combined variance is
 below some threshold

´éí Many alternatives: Treat dimensions
 differently, consider density

            J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   45
CS-404 Big Data Analytics

  Week 14 ÔÇô Clustering

    Dr. Syed Imran Ali

                            22/04/2026
                                                                                                     Vs.
´éí Problem with BFR/k-means:
 ´éž Assumes clusters are normally
   distributed in each dimension
 ´éž And axes are fixed ÔÇô ellipses at
   an angle are not OK

´éí CURE (Clustering Using REpresentatives):
 ´éž Assumes a Euclidean distance
 ´éž Allows clusters to assume any shape
 ´éž Uses a collection of representative
   points to represent clusters
             J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org         2
                                                                                            h                h

                                                                                                h
                                                                                    e                        e
                                                    e
                                                                          h                 e
                      e                                                         e       h
         e                       e                  e                                                   e

salary                                                         h
             e
                                                                            h
                                                             h
                                   h          h
         h       h   h


                                                  age




                     J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org       3
2 Pass algorithm. Pass 1:
´éí 0) Pick a random sample of points that fit in
  main memory
´éí 1) Initial clusters:
  ´éž Cluster these points hierarchically ÔÇô group
    nearest points/clusters
´éí 2) Pick representative points:
  ´éž For each cluster, pick a sample of points, as
    dispersed as possible
  ´éž From the sample, pick representatives by moving
    them (say) 20% toward the centroid of the cluster
              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   4
                                                                                            h                h

                                                                                                h
                                                                                    e                        e
                                                    e
                                                                          h                 e
                      e                                                         e       h
         e                       e                  e                                                   e
                                                               h
salary       e
                                                                            h
                                                             h
                                   h          h
         h       h   h



                                                  age


                     J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org       5
                                                                                            h                h

                                                                                                h
                                                                                    e                        e
                                                    e
                                                                          h                 e
                      e                                                         e       h
         e                       e                  e                                                   e
                                                               h
salary       e
                                                                            h                                    Pick (say) 4
                                                             h                                                   remote points
                                   h          h                                                                  for each
         h       h   h                                                                                           cluster.


                                                  age


                     J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org                       6
                                                                                            h                h

                                                                                                h
                                                                                    e                        e
                                                    e
                                                                          h                 e
                      e                                                         e       h
         e                       e                  e                                                   e
                                                               h
salary       e
                                                                            h                                    Move points
                                                             h                                                   (say) 20%
                                   h          h                                                                  toward the
         h       h   h                                                                                           centroid.


                                                  age


                     J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org                     7
Pass 2:
´éí Now, rescan the whole dataset and
  visit each point p in the data set

´éí Place it in the ÔÇťclosest clusterÔÇŁ                                                                   p
  ´éž Normal definition of ÔÇťclosestÔÇŁ:
    Find the closest representative to p and
    assign it to representativeÔÇÖs cluster




              J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   8
´éí Clustering: Given a set of points, with a notion
  of distance between points, group the points
  into some number of clusters
´éí Algorithms:
  ´éž Agglomerative hierarchical clustering:
    ´éž Centroid and clustroid
  ´éž k-means:
    ´éž Initialization, picking k
  ´éž BFR
  ´éž CURE
                J. Leskovec, A. Rajaraman, J. Ullman: Mining of Massive Datasets, http://www.mmds.org   9
